basics.name: Kenneth Zhang
basics.label: Quantitative Researcher
basics.summary: A Canadian-born Quantitative Researcher, specializing in volatility modelling, statistical arbitrage, and statistical machine learning.

work.name: Squarepoint Capital
work.summary: Options Market-Making, Futures Microstructure, Volatility Forecasting

work.name: Mackenzie Investments
work.summary: Volatility Risk Premium, Systematic Equity Options, FX Carry

work.name: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
work.position: Research Assistant to the Director of Biostatistics

education.institution: University of Waterloo, David R. Cheriton School of Computer Science

awards.title: Star-Friedman Scholars - Harvard University
awards.awarder: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
awards.summary: Awarded the Star-Friedman Challenge for Promising Scientific Research alongside Professor Tanujit Dey and Professor Francesca Dominici for promising research in statistical time series modelling and public health research.

publications.name: Lag time between state-level policy interventions and change points in COVID-19 outcomes in the United States
publications.summary: The research focused on using a data-driven search algorithm to detect change points in COVID-19 case and death trajectories, correlating these changes with state-level policy implementations.

publications.name: Association of temporary Environmental Protection Agency regulation suspension with industrial economic viability and local air quality in California, United States
publications.summary: The study used machine learning models to predict weekly employment data and t-tests to assess the economic impact of the EPA's 2020 enforcement regulation rollbacks on oil and manufacturing industries, as well as on air quality in California, ultimately finding no economic growth and continued pollution despite the suspensions.

publications.name: A Review on the Biological, Epidemiological, and Statistical Relevance of COVID-19 paired with Air Pollution
publications.summary: This narrative review critically evaluates recent studies on the associations between various air pollutants—CO, NO2, O3, PM2.5, PM10, and SO2—and COVID-19 outcomes, examining their individual and combined effects across different regions and exposure periods, while also exploring the biological mechanisms underlying these associations.
